1: Psalm 81 is a festal psalm set to the gittît tune (Ps 8; 84 — possibly from Gath or a harvest/winepress instrument). הַרְנִינוּ לֵאלֹהִים עוּזֵּנוּ (harninû lêʾlōhîm ʿuzzênû, "Sing aloud to God our strength!") — rānan: to ring out, to shout in joy (the piercing sound of festival exuberance). ʿUzzênû: "our strength" — the divine name replaced by an epithet of power (Ps 46:1). הָרִיעוּ לֵאלֹהֵי יַעֲקֹב (hārîʿû lêʾlōhê yaʿăqōb, "shout for joy to the God of Jacob!") — rûaʿ (hiphil: to raise a war-cry/shout of joy — the ambiguous root covers both). The God of Jacob (Gen 28:13-16) — the covenant made at Bethel with the wanderer who wrestled until blessing came.
2: שְׂאוּ-זִמְרָה וּתְנוּ-תֹף (śĕʾû-zimrāh ûtĕnû-tōp, "raise a song, beat the tambourine!") — tōp: the hand-drum/tambourine (Miriam's instrument at the Exodus — Exod 15:20; the women greeting David — 1 Sam 18:6). כִּנּוֹר נָעִים עִם-נָבֶל (kinnôr nāʿîm ʿim-nābel, "the sweet lyre with the harp") — kinnôr nāʿîm: "pleasant/sweet lyre" (the same root as David's epithet nĕʿîm zĕmirôt yiśrāʾêl — "the sweet singer of Israel" — 2 Sam 23:1). The full instrumental ensemble assembled for the festival.
3: תִּקְעוּ בַחֹדֶשׁ שׁוֹפָר בַּכֵּסֶה לְיוֹם חַגֵּנוּ (tiqĕʿû baḥōdesh shôpār bakkesse lĕyôm ḥaggênû, "blow the shofar at the new moon, at the full moon, on our feast day!") — baḥōdesh: at the new moon (the beginning of the month); bakkesse: at the fullness/covering (the full moon — the 15th of the month). The combination identifies either the Feast of Tabernacles (Tishri 1 = new moon; Tishri 15 = full moon) or the Passover cycle. The shôpār (ram's horn) is blown at both new moons (Num 10:10) and major festivals (Lev 23:24).
4: כִּי חֹק לְיִשְׂרָאֵל הוּא מִשְׁפָּט לֵאלֹהֵי יַעֲקֹב (kî ḥōq lĕyiśrāʾêl hûʾ mishpāṭ lêʾlōhê yaʿăqōb, "for it is a statute for Israel, a rule given by the God of Jacob") — ḥōq: a fixed ordinance (the decrees of the sacred calendar — Lev 23). Mishpāṭ: a determined ruling. The two terms emphasize fixity (ḥōq) and authority (mishpāṭ): this feast is neither optional nor human invention but divinely inscribed in the covenant calendar.
5: עֵדוּת בִּיהוֹסֵף שָׂמוֹ בְּצֵאתוֹ עַל-אֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם (ʿêdût bîhôsêp śāmô bĕṣêtô ʿal-ʾereṣ miṣrāyim, "he established it as a decree in Joseph when he went out over the land of Egypt") — the pronoun in bĕṣêtô (when he went out) is grammatically ambiguous: it could refer to Joseph rising to power, to Israel departing Egypt at the Exodus, or to God going out against Egypt in judgment (Exod 11:4). All three readings are defensible. שְׂפַת לֹא-יָדַעְתִּי אֶשְׁמָע (śĕpat lōʾ-yādaʿtî ʾeshmaʿ, "I heard a voice I had not known") — the dramatic shift into first person: the Psalmist (or personified Israel) says "I hear a language I did not know." This marks the transition into the embedded divine speech that runs through v16.
6: Divine speech begins. הֲסִירוֹתִי מִסֵּבֶל שִׁכְמוֹ (hăsîrôtî missibbel shikmô, "I removed the burden from his shoulder") — sibbel: the burden-basket (Exod 1:11 — Israel forced to carry burdens in Egypt; Exod 2:11 — Moses "saw their burdens"). Shikmo: his shoulder (the load-bearing joint). כַּפָּיו מִדּוּד תַּעֲבֹרְנָה (kappāyw middûd taʿăbōrnāh, "his hands were freed from the basket") — dûd: a large pot/basket for carrying clay bricks. The Exodus liberation described in terms of physical release from forced labor — hands freed from the brick-basket grounds the festival command in lived memory.
7: בַּצָּרָה קָרָאתָ וָאֲחַלְּצֶךָּ (baṣṣārāh qārāʾtā wāʾăḥallĕṣekā, "in your distress you called, and I rescued you!") — ṣārāh: the narrow/distressed situation (Ps 18:6). Ḥālaṣ (piel: to draw out, liberate — pulling from a tight place). אֶעֶנְךָ בְּסֵתֶר רַעַם (ʾeʿenkā bĕsêter raʿam, "I answered you from the secret/hidden place of thunder") — sêter raʿam: the dark storm-cloud from which YHWH spoke at Sinai (Exod 19:16-20; 20:18-21). אֶבְחָנְךָ עַל-מֵי מְרִיבָה סֶלָה (ʾebḥānĕkā ʿal-mê mĕrîbāh selāh, "I tested you at the waters of Meribah!") — Exod 17:7 and Num 20:13: where Israel tested God and God tested Israel (Ps 95:8-9). Selāh.
8: שְׁמַע עַמִּי וְאָעִידָה בָּךְ (shĕmaʿ ʿammî wĕʾāʿîdāh bāk, "listen, O my people, while I admonish you!") — ʿûd (hiphil: to testify against, to warn, to admonish — as a covenant witness). The divine speech becomes a covenant lawsuit: God as the witness against Israel's faithlessness. יִשְׂרָאֵל אִם-תִּשְׁמַע-לִי (yiśrāʾêl ʾim-tishmaʿ-lî, "O Israel, if you would only listen to me!") — the conditional (Deut 28 pattern): what follows are covenant terms and their consequences, not mere exhortation.
9: לֹא-יִהְיֶה בְךָ אֵל זָר (lōʾ-yihyeh bĕkā ʾêl zār, "there must be no foreign god among you!") — ʾêl zār: strange/foreign god (Deut 32:12). וְלֹא תִשְׁתַּחֲוֶה לְאֵל נֵכָר (wĕlōʾ tishtaḥăweh lĕʾêl nêkār, "you must not bow down to any alien god!") — nêkār: the foreign, alien (Ruth 2:10). Two words for foreignness (zār and nêkār) applied to idols — restating the First Commandment in different language.
10: אָנֹכִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֶיךָ הַמַּעַלְךָ מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם (ʾānōkî yĕhwāh ʾĕlōhêkā hammmaʿalkā mêʾereṣ miṣrāyim, "I am the LORD your God, who brought you up out of the land of Egypt!") — verbatim citation of the Decalogue preamble (Exod 20:2; Deut 5:6). The divine speech in the psalm quotes itself — the God who established Sinai re-presents it in the festal setting. הַרְחֶב-פִּיךָ וַאֲמַלְאֵהוּ (harḥeb-pîkā waʾămalʾêhû, "open your mouth wide, and I will fill it!") — like a parent bird feeding nestlings who open their mouths wide to receive (Ps 104:27-28). The wide-open mouth is the posture of receptive dependence — contrasted with the people's actual behavior in v11.
11: וְלֹא-שָׁמַע עַמִּי לְקוֹלִי (wĕlōʾ-shāmaʿ ʿammî lĕqôlî, "but my people did not listen to my voice") — the heartbreak of divine speech: the invitation of v10 refused by the very people addressed as "my people" (ʿammî — the covenant-ownership word). Lōʾ-shāmaʿ: the Shema inverted. וְיִשְׂרָאֵל לֹא-אָבָה לִי (wĕyiśrāʾêl lōʾ-ʾābāh lî, "Israel would not submit to me!") — ʾābāh: to be willing, to consent (Isa 1:19: "if you are willing and obedient, you shall eat the good of the land"). Israel's "not-willing" is the active rejection of the feast God offered.
12: וָאֲשַׁלְּחֵהוּ בִּשְׁרִירוּת לִבָּם (wāʾăshallĕḥêhû bishŏrîrût libbām, "so I gave them over to their stubborn hearts") — shālaḥ: to send, to release, to let go (releasing them to their own trajectory). Shĕrîrût lêb: stubbornness/hardness of heart (Deut 29:18; Jer 3:17; 7:24). Paul's parallel in Rom 1:24: παρέδωκεν αὐτοὺς ὁ θεός ("God gave them over") — the same judicial action: abandonment-to-consequence as punishment for willful rejection. יֵלְכוּ בְּמוֹעֲצוֹתֵיהֶם (yêlĕkû bĕmôʿăṣôtêhem, "to follow their own devices") — môʿēṣāh: counsel, deliberate plan (from yāʿaṣ: to counsel). Stubbornness generates its own counsels; God's release is to let the self-generated counsel run its course.
13: לוּ עַמִּי שֹׁמֵעַ לִי (lû ʿammî shōmêaʿ lî, "if only my people would listen to me!") — lû: the particle of unfulfilled wish (as in Num 14:2: "if only we had died in Egypt!" — the contrary-to-fact wishful). God expressing a divine wish that Israel had listened — genuine divine longing over uncollected faithfulness. יִשְׂרָאֵל בִּדְרָכַי יְהַלֵּכוּ (yiśrāʾêl bîdrākhay yĕhallêkû, "if only Israel would walk in my ways!") — dĕrākhay: my ways (the halakah — the walk-path of covenantal life). The divine lament over forfeited blessing.
14: כִּמְעַט אוֹיְבֵיהֶם אַכְנִיעַ (kimĕʿaṭ ʾôyĕbêhem ʾakhnîaʿ, "I would quickly subdue their enemies!") — kimĕʿaṭ: "as a small thing, soon, quickly" — subduing enemies would be a small matter for God. Kānaʿ (hiphil: to bring low, to subdue — Josh 7:12-13; Judg 4:23). וְעַל-צָרֵיהֶם אָשִׁיב יָדִי (wĕʿal-ṣārêhem ʾāshîb yādî, "and turn my hand against their foes!") — shûb yād: to turn one's hand against — the divine hand employed against Israel's enemies instead of against Israel. The counterfactual: IF Israel listened → THEN enemies quickly subdued.
15: מְשַׂנְאֵי יְהוָה יְכַחֲשׁוּ-לוֹ (mĕśanʾê yĕhwāh yĕkhaḥăshû-lô, "those who hate YHWH would cringe before him!") — kāḥash (piel: to cringe, to fawn in feigned submission — as a conquered party): the enemies who hated God would come crawling. וִיהִי עִתָּם לְעוֹלָם (wîhî ʿittām lĕʿôlām, "and Israel's time would have endured forever!") — ʿittām: their appointed time of blessing/wellbeing. The reward of obedience: Israel's peace fixed, not subject to the rise-and-fall cycles of the Judges.
16: וַיַּאֲכִלֵהוּ מֵחֵלֶב חִטָּה (wayyaʾăkhîlêhû mêḥêleb ḥiṭṭāh, "I would have fed you with the finest of the wheat!") — ḥêleb ḥiṭṭāh: "the fat/richest of the wheat" (the very finest grain — ḥêleb is the word for the best portion offered in sacrifice: Lev 3:16-17). וּמִצּוּר דְּבַשׁ אַשְׂבִּיעֶךָ (ûmiṣṣûr dĕbash ʾasbîʿekā, "and with honey from the rock I would satisfy you!") — dĕbash miṣṣûr: honey from the rock (Deut 32:13 — wild honeybees nesting in rock clefts; the wilderness providing abundance in an unexpected place). The conditional feast: had Israel listened, the finest wheat and rock-honey would have been their portion — the wilderness provisions multiplied to their ultimate form.
